Spotify is conducting big layoffs, thanks in part to debt it took out in a zero-interest rate environment. What is more problematic is that the company has little to show for its spending.



View in browser

Spotify Layoffs, Lessons From Spotify, Ek's Letter and Spotify's Debt

Wednesday, December 6, 2023

Listen to this Update in your podcast player

Good morning,

I\xe2\x80\x99ve updated the Stratechery posting schedule for 2024; all dates are, of course, subject to change.

On that note, there will only be one Sharp Tech episode this week, which will be released on Thursday; there was a sports-centric episode of Dithering yesterday.

On to the Update:

Spotify Layoffs

From Bloomberg :

Spotify Technology SA is reducing its workforce by 17% in the company\xe2\x80\x99s steepest cuts this year, part of an aggressive effort to shrink costs and drive profitability. Employees affected by the layoffs will be notified on Monday, the music streaming company said in a statement. Roughly 1,500 jobs are being eliminated, a spokesperson said. Shares surged as much as 11% on Monday to $201.42.

The streaming audio giant is on pace to add more than 100 million users in 2023 \xe2\x80\x94 its biggest year yet. And it reported a rare profit last quarter. But Chief Executive Officer Daniel Ek said in a note to employees on Monday that Spotify is still spending too much, citing an economic slowdown and the rising cost of capital.

It\xe2\x80\x99s never pleasant to write about layoffs, and my condolences to those affected. It\xe2\x80\x99s even more difficult when my duty as an analyst is to note that these layoffs were absolutely necessary and arguably years overdue.

Lessons From Spotify

Spotify would seem, at first glance, to be a company that I would be extremely bullish about. After all, they are positioned as an Aggregator in the music industry: they deliver an abundance of audio content to users and help them with discovery.

The problem, though, is that Aggregators are the most profitable in either highly fragmented industries \xe2\x80\x94 think Google and the web \xe2\x80\x94 or when they have fully integrated and monopolized their supply \xe2\x80\x94 think Facebook and social media. Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s suppliers (the music labels), on the other hand, are an oligopoly that has retained their pricing power due to integrating new music and back catalogs. I explained in The Great Unbundling :

The music industry, meanwhile, has, at least relative to newspapers, come out of the shift to the Internet in relatively good shape; while piracy drove the music labels into the arms of Apple, which unbundled the album into the song, streaming has rewarded the integration of back catalogs and new music with bundle economics: more and more users are willing to pay $10/month for access to everything, significantly increasing the average revenue per customer. The result is an industry that looks remarkably similar to the pre-Internet era:

Notice how little power Spotify and Apple Music have; neither has a sufficient user base to attract suppliers (artists) based on pure economics, in part because they don\xe2\x80\x99t have access to back catalogs. Unlike newspapers, music labels built an integration that transcends distribution.

This power manifests in Spotify having costs that rise in-line with revenue, because they have to pay the record labels. This is why I was fairly bearish about their 2018 direct stock listing; I wrote in Lessons From Spotify :

It is not exactly groundbreaking analysis to note that Spotify has significant marginal costs \xe2\x80\x94 specifically, the royalties it pays the music industry (not just record labels but also songwriters and publishers). Those are represented by Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s Cost of Revenue:

Spotify negotiated new deals with the record labels last summer that resulted in lower royalty rates in exchange for guaranteed subscriber growth and the ability for the labels to make some releases exclusive to Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s paid tier; you can see those lower rates reflected in Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s increased margins. That, though, is precisely the problem: Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s margins are completely at the mercy of the record labels, and even after the rate change, the company is not just unprofitable, its losses are growing, at least in absolute euro terms:

Moreover, it seems highly unlikely Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s Cost of Revenue will improve much in the short-term: those record deals are locked in until at least next year, and they include \xe2\x80\x9cmost-favored nation\xe2\x80\x9d provisions, which means that Spotify has to get Universal Music Group, Sony Music Entertainment, Warner Music Group, and Merlin (the representative for many independent labels), which own 85% of the music on Spotify as measured by streams, to all agree to reduce rates collectively.

Spotify has made progress in increasing its operating margin, including by leveraging its Aggregator positioning to extract more marketing spend from the record labels; what is more important is the push into podcasting , a highly fragmented market where an Aggregator had real potential to build an advertising business.

The beauty of the advertising business model is that it is, at scale, an infinitely leverageable business: Google and Meta, for example, have self-serve advertising businesses that attract revenue with zero marginal cost; achieving the scale necessary to make such a model work and building the underlying technology are massively expensive, to be sure, but that expense is, in the best case scenario, purely fixed costs that can be leveraged across an ever increasing amount of advertising dollars earned.

Over the last few years, then, Spotify has set out to spend the money necessary to achieve that scale and build that technology; here is a graph of Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s year-over-year growth in revenue, cost of revenue, research and development, sales and marketing, and general and administrative:

The early fluctuations in this graph are interesting: the primary driver are \xe2\x80\x9csocial costs\xe2\x80\x9d, one of the risk factors in Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s financial statements:

Social costs are payroll taxes associated with employee salaries and benefits, including share-based compensation that we are subject to in various countries in which we operate. This is not a withholding tax\xe2\x80\xa6When the fair market value of our ordinary shares increases on a quarter-to-quarter basis, the accrued expense for social costs will increase, and when the fair market value of ordinary shares falls, the accrued expense will become a reduction in social costs expense, all other things being equal, including the number of vested stock options and exercise price remaining constant\xe2\x80\xa6

Additionally, approximately 31% of our employees are in Sweden. With respect to our employees in Sweden, we are required to pay a 31.42% tax to the Swedish government on the profit an employee realizes on the exercise of our stock options or the vesting of our restricted stock units (\xe2\x80\x9cRSUs\xe2\x80\x9d)\xe2\x80\xa6We cannot accurately predict how many of their vested options will remain outstanding. As a result, the cash payments to the Swedish government upon the exercise of vested stock options may vary significantly from quarter to quarter.

Indeed, those peaks and valleys do roughly align to Spotify stock swings; more pertinently, those peaks and valleys smoothed out a lot over the last few years as $SPOT both lapped its big run-up in 2020 and 2021 and started to slowly decline, reaching a nadir at the end of 2022:

That, by extension, is why the 2022 run-up in costs is notable: Spotify started spending a lot more money throughout the company; unfortunately, revenue growth has continued a slow decline from its COVID highs, with a slight uptake last quarter. That is encouraging \xe2\x80\x94 Spotify had some layoffs, and spent less on marketing than last quarter, and increased growth nonetheless \xe2\x80\x94 but the problem is that this only resulted in a tiny profit after many quarters of losses:

This isn\xe2\x80\x99t fatal in isolation: Spotify has long had positive free cash flow thanks to the fact that it collects money from subscribers weeks or months before it pays out to labels and artists. I mean sure, it\xe2\x80\x99s not ideal \xe2\x80\x94 shareholders hold Spotify stock with the assumption it will make a profit eventually \xe2\x80\x94 but the company could keep plugging away indefinitely with its current cost structure. Until, that is, 2026.

Ek\xe2\x80\x99s Letter and Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s Debt

From Billboard , back in 2021:

Spotify USA intends to offer $1.3 billion in exchangeable notes due in 2026, the company announced Wednesday. In addition, Spotify USA expects to grant the initial buyers of the notes an option to purchase an additional $200 million\xe2\x80\xa6

Exactly how Spotify intends to use the money is unclear. The press release broadly states the company intends to use the proceeds for \xe2\x80\x9cgeneral corporate purposes\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 a term loose enough to cover capital expenditures, marketing or strategic acquisitions. Such a large amount suggests Spotify doesn\xe2\x80\x99t intend to use the money for everyday costs of running the company. Instead, $1.3 billion is enough to make numerous acquisitions to build out its podcasting business, for example, and have a cushion for any uncertainty its leadership foresees in 2021 and beyond.

The exchange rate for these exchangeable notes was approximately $515.20/share; needless to say, it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t appear likely that Spotify is going to hit that price anytime before 2026. That means that Spotify will have to either pay off or refinance that debt, and is important context for Ek\xe2\x80\x99s letter to employees :

I realize that for many, a reduction of this size will feel surprisingly large given the recent positive earnings report and our performance. We debated making smaller reductions throughout 2024 and 2025. Yet, considering the gap between our financial goal state and our current operational costs, I decided that a substantial action to rightsize our costs was the best option to accomplish our objectives. While I am convinced this is the right action for our company, I also understand it will be incredibly painful for our team.

To understand this decision, I think it is important to assess Spotify with a clear, objective lens. In 2020 and 2021, we took advantage of the opportunity presented by lower-cost capital and invested significantly in team expansion, content enhancement, marketing, and new verticals. These investments generally worked, contributing to Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s increased output and the platform\xe2\x80\x99s robust growth this past year. However, we now find ourselves in a very different environment. And despite our efforts to reduce costs this past year, our cost structure for where we need to be is still too big.

When we look back on 2022 and 2023, it has truly been impressive what we have accomplished. But, at the same time, the reality is much of this output was linked to having more resources. By most metrics, we were more productive but less efficient. We need to be both. While we have done some work to mitigate this challenge and become more efficient in 2023, we still have a ways to go before we are both productive and efficient.

It\xe2\x80\x99s not an accident that Ek mentions the previous low interest rate environment, which no longer exists; I\xe2\x80\x99m not sure if Spotify has a credit rating \xe2\x80\x94 I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t able to find it \xe2\x80\x94 but let\xe2\x80\x99s assume they would have to raise debt with an interest rate of 7.5%: that means annual interest payments of $97.5 million if it rolls over that full $1.3 billion. That would eat up a lot of that free cash flow, and give Spotify significantly less room to maneuver.

In short, you could make the case \xe2\x80\x94 I did! \xe2\x80\x94 that Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s costs were too high even back when the company IPO\xe2\x80\x99d, but who cares what an analyst says? You could also make the case that Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s costs have been too high given the company\xe2\x80\x99s serial inability to turn a profit, but who cares what shareholders say? You can also make the case that Spotify\xe2\x80\x99s costs are too high given the company\xe2\x80\x99s debt load, and in this case Ek absolutely needs to care what bankers say.

Still, if the debt were the only thing, then this would be an unfortunate but understandable pullback. What has to be discouraging for Ek is the fact that all of that spending never did meaningfully increase the revenue growth rate. That\xe2\x80\x99s not to say it won\xe2\x80\x99t happen \xe2\x80\x94 advertising platforms take time to build \xe2\x80\x94 but it is definitely disappointing to have made such big moves over the last two years and have so little to show for it.
